does: This paper develops a machine unlearning framework for RL-based ransomware detection systems using SISA (Sharded, Isolated, Sliced, and Aggregated) training methodology. The core contribution is enabling efficient removal of specific training samples' influence from detection models without full retraining. The authors implement and evaluate the approach using value-based RL agents (DQN and DDQN) on a Windows 11 ransomware dataset, demonstrating that retraining only affected model shards substantially reduces computational cost while maintaining detection performance. The evaluation employs cost-sensitive reward design and 5-fold cross-validation, with detection confidence assessed through continuous Q-value metrics.
connects: This work addresses the intersection of machine learning security and privacy compliance, building on established lines of research in both machine unlearning techniques and behavior-based malware detection. The application of SISA training to unlearning extends prior work on efficient model update methods originally developed for privacy-preserving machine learning. The paper responds to growing regulatory demands (GDPR, data governance frameworks) that necessitate the right to be forgotten in AI systems, a challenge previously explored primarily in supervised learning contexts but less developed for RL-based security applications. It combines reinforcement learning approaches for ransomware detection, which have gained traction as alternatives to traditional static analysis, with systematic approaches to training data deletion.
enables: This framework opens pathways for privacy-compliant deployment of RL-based security systems in regulated environments where data deletion requests must be honored efficiently. The SISA-based unlearning approach could extend to other RL security applications beyond ransomware, including intrusion detection, fraud prevention, and anomaly detection systems. It establishes methodologies for evaluating unlearning effectiveness in adversarial domains, potentially enabling certification of compliance with data protection regulations. Future work could explore unlearning under adaptive adversarial settings, develop theoretical guarantees on detection performance retention after deletion, and integrate formal verification methods to ensure that removed training samples no longer influence model predictions in security-critical scenarios.
